evidence_quotes.quote: the practice of killing off video games entirely.

paragraphs.text: First of all, as a customer and frequent user of digital services and products, I think the Digital Fairness Act sounds FANTASTIC. It addresses a large number of grievances I currently have with digital services, the likes of which will no doubt only get worse without regulations like this. Full support! However, I, like many others before me, believe there is a particular practice that also needs addressing in this act, and that is the practice of killing off video games entirely. In the write-up of the act, I believe this most closely matches dark pattern #8, Bait and Switch. Buying a game on a platform, e.g. on Steam (itself a reputable service), is presented and marketed as buying a game, rather than buying an ultimately temporary license to play the game while support lasts, the duration of which is unknown to both user and provider and, in many cases, is decided entirely by whether or not the game's existence is profitable (Ubisoft's The Crew being a very prominent example in recent years). This feels like deceptive marketing, along the lines of buying a movie disc that you can only watch for somewhere in between 1-3 years after buying, and then being told you actually only bought a license to view the movie while support lasts. On a more personal note, with me being solidly in my adult years by now, I frequently find joy and nostalgia in being able to access and play games I played in my childhood, or simply other games of similar nature, and as a hobbyist game developer, it is quite fascinating to be able to learn from these older titles! I have already experienced the shuttering of games I actually enjoyed: Auto Assault (2006-2007) and Tabula Rasa (2007-2009), to name a couple. I remember these with fondness and curiosity, but I will most likely never be able to play these again. These days, many people may grow up with video games that simply will not be able to be played in a decade from now, whereas I'm able to pull up Doom from 32 years ago, and it feels deeply unfair to these people that video games should be a unique exception to the blissful comfort of pulling out an old piece of media for a little trip back to childhood. I dearly hope that this option will be kept open for them!
